channel.title: Shreyas Korad — Writing
channel.description: Articles and insights on learning design, gamification, and L&D strategy.

item.title: Why rigid streaks backfire
item.description: Why rigid streaks backfire: Teams use visible mechanics, but the design still fails when it does not protect motivation, identity, and repeat behavior in real work.

item.title: Your Bio as an Origin Story: Gamifying the Job Hunt
item.description: Your Bio as an Origin Story: Gamifying the Job Hunt: Teams care about the topic, but the surrounding systems still produce activity without durable capability, better decisions, or visible business impact.

item.title: Points and badges are not the mechanism
item.description: Points and badges are not the mechanism: Teams use visible mechanics, but the design still fails when it does not protect motivation, identity, and repeat behavior in real work.

item.title: When the Game Is Rigged: Why Venezuela Couldn’t Win
item.description: When the Game Is Rigged: Why Venezuela Couldn’t Win: teams care about the topic, but the surrounding systems still produce activity without durable capability, better decisions, or visible business impact.

item.title: Gamification fails at the moment of choice
item.description: Gamification fails at the moment of choice: teams are treating gamification design as tooling rollout, but the real constraint is whether work systems, manager behavior, and capability-building routines actually change.

item.title: Mechanics fail when motivation design is weak
item.description: Mechanics fail when motivation design is weak: teams are treating gamification design as tooling rollout, but the real constraint is whether work systems, manager behavior, and capability-building routines actually change.

item.title: Invisible feedback loops are already shaping behavior
item.description: Invisible feedback loops are already shaping behavior: teams care about the topic, but the surrounding systems still produce activity without durable capability, better decisions, or visible business impact.

item.title: Why activity metrics are not evidence of impact
item.description: Why activity metrics are not evidence of impact: Teams care about the topic, but the surrounding systems still produce activity without durable capability, better decisions, or visible business impact.

item.title: Effort isn't the problem. Your learning system is
item.description: Most learning failures are system failures: passive methods, weak feedback, and no spaced retrieval.

item.title: Design thinking is not retention
item.description: Design thinking improves usability. Retention needs a behaviour model that makes people return.

item.title: Learning games fail when we try to teach everything
item.description: Learning games break when they try to teach everything. Design around one high value behaviour, then scale.

item.title: Delight belongs in the core loop
item.description: Delight is not garnish. Research on user experience shows that appeal comes from the interplay of pragmatic and hedonic quality during use, not from decorative extras added at the end.

item.title: Novelty is not retention
item.description: Gamification can help people start, but it rarely keeps them by itself. Longitudinal research shows that once novelty fades, retention depends on meaningful progress, tailored design, and autonomy.

item.title: Why teams reject AI that feels like replacement
item.description: AI adoption usually fails for a human reason. When the system feels like control or replacement, teams resist it. When it supports autonomy, competence, and visible wins, usage rises.

item.title: When a leaderboard becomes surveillance
item.description: Leaderboards can create short-term movement, but in workplace learning they often drift into surveillance. The better design question is whether the system strengthens autonomy, competence, and useful feedback.
